content:
The read came back in 4 milliseconds, and I screenshotted it for the channel.

8 min read
Just now

--

We’d been chasing one slow dashboard query for a week. It was a filtered, sorted lookup over an orders table — give me the last 50 orders for this merchant, in this status, newest first — and during business hours it kept crawling past our timeout and dying. The dashboard would spin, then throw a red banner, then somebody in support would ping us asking why the merchant portal was down again. It wasn’t down. It was just waiting on a query that had quietly gotten too slow to finish.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

One composite index later, it dropped from 900ms to single digits. Someone reacted with a fire emoji. I closed the ticket and moved on.
Six weeks later I got paged at 2am for replication lag, and the slowest thing in the entire system was writes to that exact table.
Here’s the part nobody tells you while you’re celebrating a fast read.
The index did exactly what we asked it to
The query planner isn’t magic, and it isn’t guessing. When you add an index, you’re telling the database to build and maintain a second, sorted structure — usually a B-tree — that holds copies of the columns…
